content.full_text:
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 3, 2010

Katherine O. Gibson
5001 California Ave, Suite 140
Bakersfield, CA 93389

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our file No. A-10-071

Dear Ms. Gibson:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Taft City Council regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that our advice is based solely on provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Also note our advice is based solely on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Based on the facts described below, do two of the council members in the City of Taft have conflicts of interest under the Act that would preclude them from participating in the evaluation of the City Attorney?

CONCLUSION

No. Neither of the councilmembers have a cognizable economic interest under the Act. The Commission does not, however, offer advice on other areas of law.

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of Taft. The Taft City Council has asked whether two of its members have conflicts of interest that would preclude them from participating in an

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-071
Page No. 2

evaluation of the City Attorney (we assume that the council will be evaluating you and your
firm). The city council's evaluation is scheduled for May 4, 2010.

Councilmember Cliff Thompson filed a $500,000 claim with the City of Taft in
December of 2009, for defamation of character and failure to comply with a Freedom of
Information Act request.² The claim named the City Manager, City Clerk, and Human
Resources Manager. The city council rejected the claim in January of 2010. Nothing in your
facts suggests that this claim is alive in any other forum.

Councilmember Craig Noble stated that he believes the City Manager interfered with the
sale of a building that Councilmember Noble owned and also that he intended to consult an
attorney on the subject. When the city council was to evaluate the City Manager, the City
Attorney recommended that Councilmember Noble recuse himself, which he did. Nothing in
your facts suggests that there is a live action in this matter.

Taft's mayor and councilmember Dave Noerr believes that these councilmember may
have a conflict of interest in participating in the city attorney evaluation based on the above.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.

Steps 1 & 2: Are the Councilmembers Public Officials Making, Participating in Making,
or Influencing a Governmental Decision?

As members of the Taft City Council, Councilmembers Noble and Thompson are public
officials under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, they may not make, participate in
making, or otherwise use their official positions to influence any decision that will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of their economic interests. Because both

___________________________
² The Freedom of Information Act is a federal act (5 U.S.C. § 552) applicable to agencies of the United
States Government and not applicable to state or local government agencies. We assume you (or, perhaps he) meant
that the claim involves an allegation of failure to comply with the California Public Records Act (Gov. Code §§
6250, et seq.).

File No. A-10-071
Page No. 3

will be called upon, in their official capacities, to evaluate the City Attorney, they will be making
participating in making, or otherwise using their official positions to influence a governmental
decision.

**Step 3: Do the Councilmembers Have a Potentially Disqualifying Economic Interest?**

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)

• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct
or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2.)

• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)

• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)

• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those
of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Based on the information you provided, neither of the councilmembers has a
cognizable economic interest, listed above. Councilmember Thompson filed a claim
against the City of Taft that the city council rejected. Even if this were an economic
interest, which it is not, there is nothing to suggest that the claim was revived in such a
way that he would have an interest of any sort in that claim. Based on your facts,
Councilmember Noble has done nothing more than threatened to consult an attorney, and
we see no economic interest in that threat.

Because there are no economic interests as provided in your facts, we do not
continue with the 8-step analysis. We do remind you, however, that the Act is the only

File No. A-10-071
Page No. 4

body of law within our jurisdiction and we therefore do not offer advice on other areas of
law.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

QUESTION: Based on the facts described below, do two of the council members in the City of Taft
have conflicts of interest under the Act that would preclude them from participating in the
evaluation of the City Attomey?

CONCLUSION: No. Neither of the councilmembers have a cognizable economic interest under the Act.
The commission does not, however, offer advice on other areas of law.
